task: I’m putting together a service agreement for a small consulting job and want to make sure I’m not leaving myself exposed if the scope changes or the client drags their feet on payment. Can you help me outline the main clauses I should be thinking about before I send it over?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — for a small consulting agreement, the main goal is to make the scope, payment terms, and change process very clear so you’re not doing extra work for free or waiting indefinitely to get paid.

Here are the key clauses to consider:

### 1) Scope of Services
Define exactly what you will do, and just as importantly, what you will not do.
- Deliverables
- Timeline or milestones
- Assumptions
- Exclusions

### 2) Change Order / Scope Creep
Add a process for any work outside the original scope.
- Client must request changes in writing
- You provide a revised estimate and timeline
- No extra work starts until both sides agree
- Additional fees apply for out-of-scope work

### 3) Fees and Payment Terms
Be very specific about how and when you get paid.
- Fixed fee, hourly rate, or retainer
- Invoice schedule
- Due date (e.g., net 15 or net 30)
- Late fees or interest on overdue amounts
- Whether expenses are reimbursable and how approved

### 4) Deposit / Upfront Payment
If appropriate, require part of the fee before starting.
- Nonrefundable deposit
- Work begins only after payment clears
- Deposit credited against final invoice

### 5) Suspension for Nonpayment
Give yourself the right to pause work if invoices aren’t paid.
- Right to suspend services after notice
- No liability for delays caused by suspension
- Timeline resumes once payment is received

### 6) Client Responsibilities
Make sure the client knows what they must provide.
- Timely feedback and approvals
- Access to information, systems, or personnel
- Accurate materials and data
- Consequences if they delay you

### 7) Acceptance / Sign-Off
Clarify how deliverables are approved.
- Review period
- Deemed acceptance if no objections within a certain time
- Limit on revision rounds, if relevant

### 8) Term and Termination
State how the agreement ends.
- Start and end date, or project completion
- Either party’s right to terminate with notice
- Payment owed for work completed up to termination
- What happens to unfinished work

### 9) Intellectual Property / Ownership
Spell out who owns the work product.
- Ownership transfers only after full payment
- You retain pre-existing materials, templates, and know-how
- Client gets a license or assignment as appropriate

### 10) Confidentiality
Protect sensitive information both ways if needed.
- What counts as confidential
- How it may be used
- Exceptions and duration

### 11) Limitation of Liability
This is a big one for reducing exposure.
- Cap liability to fees paid or another agreed amount
- Exclude indirect/consequential damages
- Carve-outs if needed for willful misconduct or fraud

### 12) Independent Contractor Status
Clarify that you’re not an employee.
- You control how the work is performed
- No benefits, taxes, or employment relationship

### 13) Dispute Resolution
Decide how disputes will be handled.
- Governing law
- Venue
- Mediation or arbitration, if desired
- Attorney’s fees clause, if appropriate

### 14) Miscellaneous Boilerplate
These help avoid ambiguity.
- Entire agreement
- Amendments in writing only
- Assignment restrictions
- Force majeure
- Severability
- Notices

## Practical protections you may want to include
If your main concerns are scope changes and slow payment, the most important clauses are:
- Clear scope/exclusions
- Written change-order requirement
- Payment due dates and late fees
- Right to stop work for nonpayment
- Payment required before final deliverables are released
- Ownership transfers only upon full payment

If you want, I can also help you turn this into a simple one-page consulting agreement outline or a plain-English clause checklist you can drop into a draft.

user_simulator.text:
Good start, but it’s still generic. I need the clauses that actually protect me when the client starts moving the goalposts or sitting on invoices.

Add these specifics:
- scope tied to a defined deliverable list, with anything not listed treated as out-of-scope
- written change orders only, and no work starts until I approve the fee/timeline change
- deposit or retainer up front, preferably before any work begins
- payment due on a short leash, not loose net-30 forever
- late fee / finance charge language
- right to suspend work immediately if payment is overdue
- no release of final deliverables until the balance clears
- client must provide records, access, and timely feedback, or deadlines move
- limit on revisions / review rounds
- terminate-for-convenience clause with payment owed for work done to date
- liability cap, because I’m not taking open-ended exposure

Also, I’d want ownership of my templates, methods, and pre-existing materials locked down. If you want, tighten this into a real clause-by-clause outline I can actually use.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely. Here’s a tighter, protection-focused outline you can use as the backbone of the agreement.

## 1) Scope of Services
Tie the work to a **specific deliverable list** and make everything else out-of-scope.

**Include:**
- A numbered list of deliverables
- What each deliverable includes
- Any assumptions
- Explicit exclusions

**Key language idea:**
- “Services are limited to the deliverables listed in Exhibit A. Any task, revision, meeting, analysis, or output not expressly listed is out of scope unless approved in a written change order.”

---

## 2) Change Orders / Scope Creep
Make this strict: **written only, no exceptions, no work until approved.**

**Include:**
- Client must request changes in writing
- You provide revised fee/timeline
- No obligation to perform extra work until both sides sign off or otherwise approve in writing
- Out-of-scope work billed separately

**Key language idea:**
- “Consultant has no obligation to begin any out-of-scope work until the parties agree in writing to the revised fees, schedule, and deliverables.”

---

## 3) Deposit / Retainer Up Front
Require money before you start.

**Include:**
- Deposit or retainer due on signing
- Work does not begin until cleared funds are received
- Deposit applied to final fees, if that’s your preference
- If you want it stronger, make it nonrefundable to reserve your time

**Key language idea:**
- “Consultant will not commence services until the deposit/retainer has been paid in full and cleared.”

---

## 4) Payment Terms
Short leash, not open-ended net-30.

**Include:**
- Invoice due date: e.g. **due upon receipt**, **net 7**, or **net 10**
- No work continues if an invoice is overdue
- Optional milestone billing or weekly invoicing for hourly work
- Expenses only if pre-approved in writing

**Key language idea:**
- “All invoices are due within 7 days of invoice date unless otherwise stated in writing.”

---

## 5) Late Fees / Finance Charges
This is where you protect against slow pay.

**Include:**
- Late fee or finance charge on overdue balances
- Interest accrues from the due date
- Reimbursement of collection costs and reasonable attorney’s fees, if you want that added

**Key language idea:**
- “Overdue amounts accrue a finance charge of 1.5% per month or the maximum rate permitted by law, whichever is less.”

---

## 6) Right to Suspend Work for Nonpayment
This should be immediate or very fast.

**Include:**
- Right to pause work if payment is late
- No liability for delays caused by suspension
- Deadlines extend automatically while payment is overdue
- Resume only after all overdue amounts are paid

**Key language idea:**
- “If any invoice remains unpaid when due, Consultant may immediately suspend services without liability until all overdue amounts are paid in full.”

---

## 7) Final Deliverables Held Until Payment Clears
Do not release the final product until you’re paid.

**Include:**
- Final deliverables, source files, or handoff materials withheld until full payment
- No license/ownership transfer until payment clears, if that fits your model

**Key language idea:**
- “Final deliverables will not be released until all outstanding fees, charges, and reimbursable expenses have been paid in full and cleared.”

---

## 8) Client Responsibilities
Make delays their problem if they fail to cooperate.

**Include:**
- Timely access to records, systems, personnel, and materials
- Timely feedback and approvals
- Accurate information
- Deadlines shift if they delay you

**Key language idea:**
- “Client’s failure to provide requested materials, access, or feedback in a timely manner will extend all deadlines by a period reasonably necessary to account for the delay.”

---

## 9) Revision Limits / Review Rounds
This is essential if the client likes endless tweaks.

**Include:**
- Limit number of revision rounds
- Define what counts as a revision versus a new request
- Additional rounds billed separately

**Key language idea:**
- “Fees include up to two rounds of revisions per deliverable. Additional revisions or material changes are billable at the agreed hourly rate or subject to a change order.”

---

## 10) Termination for Convenience
Let either side walk away, but make sure you get paid for work already done.

**Include:**
- Either party may terminate with written notice
- Client pays for all work performed through termination date
- If you’ve reserved time or incurred non-cancellable costs, include those too
- Optional wind-down period

**Key language idea:**
- “Upon termination, Client remains responsible for all fees incurred through the effective termination date, plus approved expenses and any non-cancellable commitments made by Consultant.”

---

## 11) Limitation of Liability
This is your main exposure cap.

**Include:**
- Liability capped at fees paid or payable under the agreement
- Exclude consequential, incidental, special, or punitive damages
- Consider carve-outs only if required by law

**Key language idea:**
- “Consultant’s total liability arising out of or related to this agreement shall not exceed the fees actually paid by Client under this agreement.”

---

## 12) Ownership / Intellectual Property
Protect your templates, methods, and pre-existing materials.

**Include:**
- You retain ownership of pre-existing materials, templates, tools, know-how, and methodologies
- Client gets rights only to the final paid deliverables, if applicable
- Any assignment of work product happens only after full payment
- You can reuse general skills and non-client-specific know-how

**Key language idea:**
- “Consultant retains all right, title, and interest in pre-existing materials, templates, methodologies, tools, and know-how. Client receives no rights in such materials except as expressly granted in writing.”

---

# If you want the strongest practical version, the core protections are:
1. **Defined deliverables only**
2. **Written change orders only**
3. **Upfront deposit/retainer**
4. **Short payment deadline**
5. **Late fees**
6. **Immediate suspension right**
7. **No final release until paid**
8. **Client delay extends deadlines**
9. **Revision cap**
10. **Termination with payment for work done**
11. **Liability cap**
12. **You keep ownership of templates/methods/pre-existing materials**

If you want, I can turn this into:
- a **clean clause-by-clause outline**, or
- **draft contract language** you can paste into your agreement.

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still need it in a format I can actually lift into a draft, not just headings.

Do it clause-by-clause with tighter language under each one — especially:
- deliverables defined as the full scope, anything not listed is out
- written change orders only, no work until I sign off on fee/timeline
- deposit/retainer due before work starts
- payment due in 7 days, not the usual mushy net-30
- late fee / finance charge
- immediate suspension for nonpayment
- no final handoff until paid in full
- client delays extend deadlines automatically
- revision cap with extra rounds billed separately
- termination for convenience, but I still get paid through the cutoff date
- liability cap
- ownership carve-out for my templates, methods, tools, and pre-existing materials

Keep it practical and contract-ready. No lecture.

turns.agent_response:
Below is a practical, contract-ready clause outline you can lift into a draft and tailor to your deal.

---

## 1) Scope of Services / Deliverables
**Clause draft:**

**Services.** Consultant shall perform only the services and deliver only the deliverables expressly listed in **Exhibit A** (the “Deliverables”). The Deliverables constitute the full scope of Consultant’s obligations under this Agreement. Any task, service, meeting, analysis, revision, advice, file, format, or output not expressly listed in Exhibit A is outside the scope of this Agreement and shall not be required unless added by a written change order signed by both parties.

---

## 2) Change Orders / Out-of-Scope Work
**Clause draft:**

**Changes to Scope.** Any requested change to the Deliverables, timeline, assumptions, specifications, review process, or other scope element must be made in writing. Consultant will have no obligation to begin any out-of-scope work unless and until Consultant has approved the revised fees and schedule in writing. If Consultant agrees to perform any out-of-scope work, the parties will document the change in a written change order or amended statement of work signed by both parties. Any such work will be billed separately.

---

## 3) Deposit / Retainer
**Clause draft:**

**Deposit.** Client shall pay a deposit/retainer of $[ თანხა ] upon execution of this Agreement. Consultant will not begin work until the deposit/retainer has been received in full and cleared. The deposit/retainer will [be credited against the final invoice / be nonrefundable as a reservation fee / be applied as specified here].

---

## 4) Payment Terms
**Clause draft:**

**Payment Due Date.** Consultant will invoice Client [upon signing / upon milestone completion / weekly / monthly]. All invoices are due and payable within **seven (7) days** of the invoice date unless otherwise stated in writing by Consultant. Payment must be made in immediately available funds. Client may not withhold, offset, or delay payment for any reason except for a good-faith dispute of a specific invoiced amount communicated in writing before the due date.

---

## 5) Late Fees / Finance Charges
**Clause draft:**

**Late Charges.** Any amount not paid when due will accrue interest or a finance charge at the rate of **1.5% per month** (or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, if lower), calculated from the due date until paid in full. Client shall also reimburse Consultant for reasonable costs of collection, including reasonable attorneys’ fees and expenses, to the extent permitted by law.

---

## 6) Suspension for Nonpayment
**Clause draft:**

**Suspension of Services.** If any invoice remains unpaid when due, Consultant may immediately suspend performance of all or any part of the Services without liability. During any suspension, all deadlines and delivery dates will be tolled and extended for a period equal to the suspension period, plus any additional time reasonably required to resume performance. Consultant will resume work only after all overdue amounts, including late charges and reimbursable costs, have been paid in full.

---

## 7) Final Handoff / Release of Deliverables
**Clause draft:**

**Final Payment شرط.** Consultant shall have no obligation to deliver, release, transfer, or provide access to final deliverables, source files, editable files, working files, or other handoff materials until all fees, late charges, reimbursable expenses, and other amounts due under this Agreement have been paid in full and cleared. If ownership or license rights are being transferred, no such transfer will occur until payment is received in full.

---

## 8) Client Responsibilities / Delay by Client
**Clause draft:**

**Client Cooperation.** Client shall timely provide all records, data, materials, access credentials, approvals, decisions, and feedback reasonably requested by Consultant to perform the Services. Client shall ensure that all information provided is complete and accurate. Any delay by Client in providing requested items or approvals will automatically extend Consultant’s deadlines and delivery dates by a period reasonably necessary to account for the delay. Consultant will not be responsible for delays, errors, or additional costs caused by Client’s failure to cooperate.

---

## 9) Revision Limit / Review Rounds
**Clause draft:**

**Revisions.** The fees under this Agreement include up to **[one/two]** rounds of revisions for each Deliverable, provided the revisions are consistent with the approved scope. Any additional revision rounds, material changes, or requests that go beyond the included review rounds will be treated as out-of-scope work and billed separately at Consultant’s then-current hourly rate or under a written change order, at Consultant’s election.

---

## 10) Termination for Convenience
**Clause draft:**

**Termination for Convenience.** Either party may terminate this Agreement for any reason upon written notice to the other party. Upon termination, Client shall pay Consultant for all Services performed and all approved expenses incurred through the effective termination date, plus any non-cancellable commitments or obligations reasonably incurred by Consultant in reliance on this Agreement. If fees are billed on a milestone or fixed-fee basis, Consultant shall be entitled to payment on a pro rata basis for work completed as of the termination date, unless the parties agree otherwise in writing.

---

## 11) Limitation of Liability
**Clause draft:**

**Limitation of Liability.** To the maximum extent permitted by law, Consultant’s total aggregate liability arising out of or relating to this Agreement, whether in contract, tort, or otherwise, shall not exceed the total fees actually paid by Client to Consultant under this Agreement. In no event shall Consultant be liable for any indirect, incidental, special, consequential, exemplary, or punitive damages, including lost profits or lost business, even if advised of the possibility of such damages.

---

## 12) Ownership / Pre-Existing Materials Carve-Out
**Clause draft:**

**Consultant Materials.** Consultant retains all right, title, and interest in and to all pre-existing materials, templates, tools, methods, processes, workflows, frameworks, know-how, software, code, documents, and other materials developed or owned by Consultant before the Effective Date or developed independently of this Agreement (“Consultant Materials”). To the extent any Consultant Materials are incorporated into the Deliverables, Consultant grants Client only a non-exclusive, non-transferable license to use such Consultant Materials solely as part of the Deliverables, and only upon full payment of all amounts due. Nothing in this Agreement transfers ownership of Consultant Materials to Client.

---

## Optional short “safety net” sentence
You can also add this to the scope section or a general clause:

**Any ambiguity in scope will be interpreted narrowly in favor of the Deliverables expressly listed in Exhibit A.**

---

If you want, I can turn this into a clean **one-page agreement skeleton** with section headings and placeholders filled in.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is usable. The clause language is tight enough that I can lift most of it straight into a draft. The narrow-scope sentence is good too — that’s the kind of thing that keeps people from pretending “one quick call” turned into a full engagement.

I’d still probably tweak the payment section to make suspension kick in automatically after the 7 days, not just as a maybe. But overall, this is the right level and the right structure.
